content:
[S1]: Compound interest is like magic money that grows faster and faster over time.  
[READER]: Wow, magic money sounds cool! But how can money grow by itself? I want to know how that works.  
[S2]: When you save money in a bank, the bank pays you extra money called interest for keeping your money there.  
[READER]: Oh, so the bank gives me money just for saving? That sounds interesting! What happens next?  
[S3]: The special thing about compound interest is that you earn interest not only on the money you saved but also on the interest you already earned.  
[READER]: Hmm, that sounds a bit tricky. So the interest grows on itself? That’s different! Can you give me an example?  
[S4]: Sure! Imagine you save $10, and after one year, you get $1 as interest, so now you have $11.  
[READER]: Okay, I get that part. But what happens after the next year?  
[S5]: Next year, you get interest on $11, not just your original $10, so you earn a little more than before.  
[READER]: Oh, so the money keeps getting bigger because I’m getting interest on the extra money too! That sounds fun!  
[S6]: Exactly! And if you keep saving and leaving your money in the bank, it will keep growing faster and faster over many years.  
[READER]: Wow, that means saving money early can make it grow a lot! I want to save now!  
[S7]: That’s right! The longer you save, the more compound interest helps your money grow like a snowball getting bigger as it rolls.  
[READER]: I love the snowball idea! Saving money is like making a giant snowball of cash!  
[S8]: So, compound interest is a smart way to make your savings grow by earning interest on top of interest.  
[READER]: Thanks! Now I understand how my money can grow like magic if I save it and wait.  

===BEGIN===  
Compound interest is like magic money that grows faster and faster over time.  
When you save money in a bank, the bank pays you extra money called interest for keeping your money there.  
The special thing about compound interest is that you earn interest not only on the money you saved but also on the interest you already earned.  
Sure! Imagine you save $10, and after one year, you get $1 as interest, so now you have $11.  
Next year, you get interest on $11, not just your original $10, so you earn a little more than before.  
Oh, so the money keeps getting bigger because I’m getting interest on the extra money too! That sounds fun!  
Exactly! And if you keep saving and leaving your money in the bank, it will keep growing faster and faster over many years.  
That’s right! The longer you save, the more compound interest helps your money grow like a snowball getting bigger as it rolls.  
So, compound interest is a smart way to make your savings grow by earning interest on top of interest.  
===END===
